mcqs.question: In a single-level directory structure, where all files are stored in a single directory, what is the primary limitation?

options.text: Files cannot be deleted once created
All files must have unique names regardless of user
The system cannot support subdirectories
File access speed is significantly reduced

mcqs.explanation: In a single-level directory, all files reside in one directory, so file names must be unique system-wide to avoid conflicts.

mcqs.question: What is the main advantage of a two-level directory structure over a single-level directory?

options.text: It allows symbolic links between files
Each user can have their own private directory with independent file names
It reduces disk space consumption
It enables sharing of files across users

mcqs.explanation: Two-level directories separate files by user, allowing each user to have their own directory where file names need only be unique within that user's directory.

mcqs.question: Consider a hierarchical directory structure in an OS. If the current working directory is /home/user/docs and the user executes 'cd ../images', what will be the new working directory?

options.text: /home/user/images
/home/images
/home/user/docs/images
/images

mcqs.explanation: The '..' moves up one level from /home/user/docs to /home/user, then 'images' descends into the images subdirectory, resulting in /home/user/images.

mcqs.question: In a tree-structured directory system, what happens when a symbolic link (shortcut) creates a cycle in the directory tree?

options.text: The OS automatically removes the cycle
It enables faster file access times
Recursive operations like tree traversal may enter infinite loops without proper cycle detection
The file system automatically converts it to a hard link

mcqs.explanation: Symbolic links can create cycles in the directory tree, causing recursive operations to loop infinitely unless the OS implements cycle detection mechanisms.

mcqs.question: Which directory operation is used to remove an empty directory, and what happens if the directory is not empty?

options.text: rmdir; it removes all contents automatically
rmdir; it returns an error if directory contains files
delete; it recursively removes directory and contents
remove; it renames the directory to a backup name

mcqs.explanation: The rmdir command specifically removes empty directories; if the directory contains files, the OS returns an error to prevent accidental data loss.

mcqs.question: In a network directory structure (like NFS), what is the primary challenge compared to local file systems?

options.text: Network directories cannot store large files
They require more disk space for metadata
Cache consistency between clients and server becomes critical
They do not support hierarchical structures

mcqs.explanation: Network directories face cache consistency issues because multiple clients may cache file data locally, and changes by one client must be properly synchronized with the server and other clients.

mcqs.question: A file system uses a linear list to implement directories. If there are N files in a directory and the file to be searched is at the end of the list, what is the time complexity of searching for a file by name?

options.text: O(log N)
O(N)
O(N²)

mcqs.explanation: A linear list implementation requires sequential searching from the beginning until the file is found, resulting in O(N) time complexity in the worst case.

mcqs.question: In a UNIX-like operating system, the absolute path '/usr/local/bin/python' and relative path '../bin/python' from '/home/user/scripts' refer to the same file. What does the '..' represent in the relative path?

options.text: The current directory
The parent directory of the current working directory
The root directory of the file system
The home directory of the current user

mcqs.explanation: The '..' notation represents the parent directory, moving up one level from /home/user/scripts to /home/user, then into the bin subdirectory to access python.
